problem_text: Пусть \(P(x)\) — такой многочлен с вещественными коэффициентами, что \(2P(m)-P(m+1)\) является целым числом для любого \(m\in\mathbb Z\). Докажите, что \(P(m)\in\mathbb Z\) для любого \(m\in\mathbb Z\).

problem.title: Многочлен
problem.statement: Пусть \(P(x)\) — такой многочлен с вещественными коэффициентами, что \(2P(m)-P(m+1)\) является целым числом для любого \(m\in\mathbb Z\). Докажите, что \(P(m)\in\mathbb Z\) для любого \(m\in\mathbb Z\).
problem.solution:
Обозначим \(\Delta P(x)=P(x+1)-P(x)\). Условие переписывается как
\[
Q(x):=2P(x)-P(x+1)=P(x)-\Delta P(x),
\]
причём \(Q(m)\in\mathbb Z\) для всех \(m\in\mathbb Z\).

**Решение 1.** Пусть \(\deg P=n\), тогда \(\Delta^{n+1}P\equiv0\). Применяя оператор \(\Delta^k\) к тождеству \(Q=P-\Delta P\), получаем
\[
\Delta^kQ=\Delta^kP-\Delta^{k+1}P
\]
при каждом \(k\ge0\). Складывая эти равенства для \(k=0,1,\ldots,n\), телескопическая сумма даёт
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n}\Delta^kQ=P-\Delta^{n+1}P=P.
\]
Поскольку \(Q(m)\in\mathbb Z\), целыми будут и все его конечные разности \(\Delta^kQ(m)\), а значит, \(P(m)\) — сумма целых чисел, то есть \(P(m)\in\mathbb Z\) для любого \(m\in\mathbb Z\).

**Решение 2.** Индукция по \(n=\deg P\).

При \(n=0\) многочлен \(P\equiv c\) и \(c=Q(0)\in\mathbb Z\).

Пусть \(n\ge1\) и утверждение верно для меньших степеней. Применяя \(\Delta\) к \(Q=P-\Delta P\), видим, что \(\Delta Q\) — результат той же операции \(R\mapsto R-\Delta R\), применённой к \(\Delta P\). При этом \(\Delta Q(m)\in\mathbb Z\) и \(\deg\Delta P=n-1\), поэтому по предположению индукции \(\Delta P(m)\in\mathbb Z\) для всех \(m\), и тогда
\[
P(m)=Q(m)+\Delta P(m)\in\mathbb Z.
\]

**Решение 3.** Запишем \(P\) в биномиальном базисе
\[
\binom{x}{k}=\frac{x(x-1)\cdots(x-k+1)}{k!}:
\qquad
P(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{d}a_k\binom{x}{k},\qquad d=\deg P.
\]
Из
\[
\binom{x+1}{k}=\binom{x}{k}+\binom{x}{k-1}
\]
следует
\[
2\binom{x}{k}-\binom{x+1}{k}=\binom{x}{k}-\binom{x}{k-1},
\]
поэтому, при \(a_{d+1}:=0\),
\[
Q(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{d}(a_k-a_{k+1})\binom{x}{k}.
\]
Многочлен принимает целые значения во всех целых точках в точности тогда, когда все его коэффициенты в этом базисе целые. Применяя это к \(Q\), получаем \(a_k-a_{k+1}\in\mathbb Z\); по нисходящей индукции \(a_d,a_{d-1},\ldots,a_0\in\mathbb Z\), значит и \(P(m)\in\mathbb Z\) для всех \(m\in\mathbb Z\).

system_prompt.text: Ты решаешь олимпиадные задачи строго и аккуратно. Сначала формализуй условие, введи обозначения и проверь все ограничения. Затем дай полное доказательство без скачков, перебора несуществующих случаев и неподтвержденных утверждений. Если используешь лемму, докажи ее. Не доказывай утверждение частным примером, если требуется доказательство для всех случаев. Перед финальным ответом проверь, что выбранные объекты действительно существуют при любых допустимых данных. В конце явно сформулируй финальный вывод. Не используй инструменты, поиск, код или калькуляторы.

messages.content: Ты решаешь олимпиадные задачи строго и аккуратно. Сначала формализуй условие, введи обозначения и проверь все ограничения. Затем дай полное доказательство без скачков, перебора несуществующих случаев и неподтвержденных утверждений. Если используешь лемму, докажи ее. Не доказывай утверждение частным примером, если требуется доказательство для всех случаев. Перед финальным ответом проверь, что выбранные объекты действительно существуют при любых допустимых данных. В конце явно сформулируй финальный вывод. Не используй инструменты, поиск, код или калькуляторы.

Пусть \(P(x)\) — такой многочлен с вещественными коэффициентами, что \(2P(m)-P(m+1)\) является целым числом для любого \(m\in\mathbb Z\). Докажите, что \(P(m)\in\mathbb Z\) для любого \(m\in\mathbb Z\).

results.answer:
### Setup and notation.

Let \( P(x) \) be a polynomial with real coefficients, say of degree at most \( n \). Write \( Q(x) := 2P(x) - P(x+1) \), which is likewise a polynomial of degree at most \( n \). By hypothesis, \( Q(m) \in \mathbb{Z} \) for every integer \( m \). Our goal is to show that \( P(m) \in \mathbb{Z} \) for every integer \( m \).

### The Newton binomial basis.

As is standard, the polynomials \( \binom{x}{k} = \frac{x(x-1) \cdots (x-k+1)}{k!} \) (for \( k = 0, \dots, n \), with the usual convention \( \binom{x}{k} = 0 \) for \( k < 0 \)) form a basis for the vector space of polynomials of degree at most \( n \). Thus we may uniquely express \( P(x) = \sum_{k=0}^n c_k \binom{x}{k} \) with coefficients \( c_k \in \mathbb{R} \). (Here \( c_{n+1} \) will be interpreted as zero in what follows.) Similarly, write \( Q(x) = \sum_{k=0}^n d_k \binom{x}{k} \) with coefficients \( d_k \in \mathbb{R} \).

### Relating the coefficients of \( P \) and \( Q \).

We first compute the coefficients \( d_k \) in terms of the \( c_k \). To this end, expand \( P(x+1) = \sum_{k=0}^n c_k \binom{x+1}{k} \). Invoking Pascal's identity \( \binom{x+1}{k} = \binom{x}{k} + \binom{x}{k-1} \), we obtain \( P(x+1) = \sum_{k=0}^n c_k \left( \binom{x}{k} + \binom{x}{k-1} \right) = \sum_{k=0}^n c_k \binom{x}{k} + \sum_{k=0}^n c_k \binom{x}{k-1} \). The second sum here reduces to \( \sum_{k=1}^n c_k \binom{x}{k-1} \) (the \( k=0 \) term vanishing), or upon reindexing \( j = k-1 \), to \( \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} c_{j+1} \binom{x}{j} \). Consequently, the coefficient of \( \binom{x}{k} \) in \( P(x+1) \) is \( c_k + c_{k+1} \) for \( 0 \leq k \leq n-1 \), and simply \( c_n \) for \( k = n \).

On the other hand, the coefficients in \( 2P(x) \) are plainly \( 2c_k \). Subtracting therefore yields the coefficients \( d_k \) of \( Q(x) \): specifically, \( d_k = 2c_k - (c_k + c_{k+1}) = c_k - c_{k+1} \) for \( 0 \leq k \leq n-1 \), while \( d_n = 2c_n - c_n = c_n \).

### The coefficients \( d_k \) are integers.

We next show that each \( d_k \) is in fact an integer. To this end, recall the forward difference operator \( \Delta f(x) = f(x+1) - f(x) \), with higher iterates denoted \( \Delta^r \). A routine verification (or induction on \( k \)) gives the action on the binomial basis: \( \Delta \binom{x}{k} = \binom{x}{k-1} \) for \( k \geq 1 \). Applying this to \( Q \), we find \( \Delta Q(x) = \sum_{k=1}^n d_k \binom{x}{k-1} = \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} d_{j+1} \binom{x}{j} \). Iterating, it follows in general that \( \Delta^r Q(x) = \sum_{k=r}^n d_k \binom{x}{k-r} \).

In particular, evaluating at \( x = 0 \), we obtain \( \Delta^r Q(0) = d_r \): indeed, the sole contributing term is that with \( k = r \), which supplies \( \binom{0}{0} = 1 \), while terms with \( k > r \) vanish since \( \binom{0}{k-r} = 0 \). Now observe that since \( Q \) takes integer values on \( \mathbb{Z} \), the difference \( \Delta Q \) is likewise integer-valued on \( \mathbb{Z} \) (as \( \Delta Q(m) = Q(m+1) - Q(m) \) is the difference of two integers, for \( m \in \mathbb{Z} \)). By induction on \( r \), the higher differences \( \Delta^r Q \) are likewise integer-valued on \( \mathbb{Z} \). In particular, \( \Delta^r Q(0) \in \mathbb{Z} \) for each \( r \), whence \( d_r \in \mathbb{Z} \) for \( r = 0, \dots, n \).

### Chaining the relations to conclude integrality of the \( c_k \).

We now have \( d_n = c_n \in \mathbb{Z} \). From the relation \( d_{n-1} = c_{n-1} - c_n \in \mathbb{Z} \), it follows immediately that \( c_{n-1} = d_{n-1} + c_n \in \mathbb{Z} \). Continuing inductively downwards, the relation \( d_{n-2} = c_{n-2} - c_{n-1} \in \mathbb{Z} \) yields \( c_{n-2} = d_{n-2} + c_{n-1} \in \mathbb{Z} \), and so on. In this way we obtain \( c_k \in \mathbb{Z} \) for each \( k = 0, \dots, n \).

### Conclusion.

With all coefficients \( c_k \) now integral, we have \( P(x) = \sum_{k=0}^n c_k \binom{x}{k} \) with \( c_k \in \mathbb{Z} \). For any integer \( m \), it remains only to observe that \( \binom{m}{k} \in \mathbb{Z} \) (for each \( k \geq 0 \)). This holds for \( m \geq 0 \) by the usual interpretation of binomial coefficients, while for \( m < 0 \) we have the identity \( \binom{m}{k} = (-1)^k \binom{k - m - 1}{k} \), which is visibly integral. Thus \( P(m) = \sum_{k=0}^n c_k \binom{m}{k} \in \mathbb{Z} \), as desired.

(Note that the argument applies equally in the event that the degree of \( P \) is strictly less than \( n \), as then the superfluous leading coefficients \( c_k \) vanish in any case.) 

This completes the proof.

message.content:
### Setup and notation.

Let \( P(x) \) be a polynomial with real coefficients, say of degree at most \( n \). Write \( Q(x) := 2P(x) - P(x+1) \), which is likewise a polynomial of degree at most \( n \). By hypothesis, \( Q(m) \in \mathbb{Z} \) for every integer \( m \). Our goal is to show that \( P(m) \in \mathbb{Z} \) for every integer \( m \).

### The Newton binomial basis.

As is standard, the polynomials \( \binom{x}{k} = \frac{x(x-1) \cdots (x-k+1)}{k!} \) (for \( k = 0, \dots, n \), with the usual convention \( \binom{x}{k} = 0 \) for \( k < 0 \)) form a basis for the vector space of polynomials of degree at most \( n \). Thus we may uniquely express \( P(x) = \sum_{k=0}^n c_k \binom{x}{k} \) with coefficients \( c_k \in \mathbb{R} \). (Here \( c_{n+1} \) will be interpreted as zero in what follows.) Similarly, write \( Q(x) = \sum_{k=0}^n d_k \binom{x}{k} \) with coefficients \( d_k \in \mathbb{R} \).

### Relating the coefficients of \( P \) and \( Q \).

We first compute the coefficients \( d_k \) in terms of the \( c_k \). To this end, expand \( P(x+1) = \sum_{k=0}^n c_k \binom{x+1}{k} \). Invoking Pascal's identity \( \binom{x+1}{k} = \binom{x}{k} + \binom{x}{k-1} \), we obtain \( P(x+1) = \sum_{k=0}^n c_k \left( \binom{x}{k} + \binom{x}{k-1} \right) = \sum_{k=0}^n c_k \binom{x}{k} + \sum_{k=0}^n c_k \binom{x}{k-1} \). The second sum here reduces to \( \sum_{k=1}^n c_k \binom{x}{k-1} \) (the \( k=0 \) term vanishing), or upon reindexing \( j = k-1 \), to \( \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} c_{j+1} \binom{x}{j} \). Consequently, the coefficient of \( \binom{x}{k} \) in \( P(x+1) \) is \( c_k + c_{k+1} \) for \( 0 \leq k \leq n-1 \), and simply \( c_n \) for \( k = n \).

On the other hand, the coefficients in \( 2P(x) \) are plainly \( 2c_k \). Subtracting therefore yields the coefficients \( d_k \) of \( Q(x) \): specifically, \( d_k = 2c_k - (c_k + c_{k+1}) = c_k - c_{k+1} \) for \( 0 \leq k \leq n-1 \), while \( d_n = 2c_n - c_n = c_n \).

### The coefficients \( d_k \) are integers.

We next show that each \( d_k \) is in fact an integer. To this end, recall the forward difference operator \( \Delta f(x) = f(x+1) - f(x) \), with higher iterates denoted \( \Delta^r \). A routine verification (or induction on \( k \)) gives the action on the binomial basis: \( \Delta \binom{x}{k} = \binom{x}{k-1} \) for \( k \geq 1 \). Applying this to \( Q \), we find \( \Delta Q(x) = \sum_{k=1}^n d_k \binom{x}{k-1} = \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} d_{j+1} \binom{x}{j} \). Iterating, it follows in general that \( \Delta^r Q(x) = \sum_{k=r}^n d_k \binom{x}{k-r} \).

In particular, evaluating at \( x = 0 \), we obtain \( \Delta^r Q(0) = d_r \): indeed, the sole contributing term is that with \( k = r \), which supplies \( \binom{0}{0} = 1 \), while terms with \( k > r \) vanish since \( \binom{0}{k-r} = 0 \). Now observe that since \( Q \) takes integer values on \( \mathbb{Z} \), the difference \( \Delta Q \) is likewise integer-valued on \( \mathbb{Z} \) (as \( \Delta Q(m) = Q(m+1) - Q(m) \) is the difference of two integers, for \( m \in \mathbb{Z} \)). By induction on \( r \), the higher differences \( \Delta^r Q \) are likewise integer-valued on \( \mathbb{Z} \). In particular, \( \Delta^r Q(0) \in \mathbb{Z} \) for each \( r \), whence \( d_r \in \mathbb{Z} \) for \( r = 0, \dots, n \).

### Chaining the relations to conclude integrality of the \( c_k \).

We now have \( d_n = c_n \in \mathbb{Z} \). From the relation \( d_{n-1} = c_{n-1} - c_n \in \mathbb{Z} \), it follows immediately that \( c_{n-1} = d_{n-1} + c_n \in \mathbb{Z} \). Continuing inductively downwards, the relation \( d_{n-2} = c_{n-2} - c_{n-1} \in \mathbb{Z} \) yields \( c_{n-2} = d_{n-2} + c_{n-1} \in \mathbb{Z} \), and so on. In this way we obtain \( c_k \in \mathbb{Z} \) for each \( k = 0, \dots, n \).

### Conclusion.

With all coefficients \( c_k \) now integral, we have \( P(x) = \sum_{k=0}^n c_k \binom{x}{k} \) with \( c_k \in \mathbb{Z} \). For any integer \( m \), it remains only to observe that \( \binom{m}{k} \in \mathbb{Z} \) (for each \( k \geq 0 \)). This holds for \( m \geq 0 \) by the usual interpretation of binomial coefficients, while for \( m < 0 \) we have the identity \( \binom{m}{k} = (-1)^k \binom{k - m - 1}{k} \), which is visibly integral. Thus \( P(m) = \sum_{k=0}^n c_k \binom{m}{k} \in \mathbb{Z} \), as desired.

(Note that the argument applies equally in the event that the degree of \( P \) is strictly less than \( n \), as then the superfluous leading coefficients \( c_k \) vanish in any case.) 

This completes the proof.
